DDP is a lot more dynamic and we'll rounded on the ground than Strickland yet he had absolutely nothing to offer Khamzat. His entire strategy once he realized Khamzat was too strong was to just lose rounds, stay safe and hope for an opportunity for a hail mary finish once Khamzat tired himself out. But lets be serious, even if his counter wrestling and TDD is on point for this fight, hes probably still gonna be on his back at some point in every round.

We've already seen Strickland vs. DDP fight for almost an entire hour. They seem pretty evenly matched if you asked me. I just dont see what Strickland is really offering Khamzat in terms of a threat, especially after Khamzats displayed pretty good cardio in his last fight. Thats the one thing Strickland can weaponize against his opponents and its not gonna work here. Khamzats last performance may have been boring as hell but it was still a complete and utter domination of someone who's already beaten Strickland twice.

posts.author: Belal Sucks
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Chimaev vs. Strickland
Sean Strickland, TKO, R4
Here the ****** thing you guys.... Strickland doesnt lose to guys that ******** to cartoons or terrorists! We got a motivated Strickland back in a title fight and honestly I think theres a good chance he can pull this off. Khamzat is an emotional fighter with a fragile ego. If Sean can get in his head in the build up I really feel like it could cause him to want to prove his skills on the feet and thrown down like he did against Gilbert Burns. If that happens I think Sean can honestly piece him up and finish him and even if he does wrestle I think Sean can work his way back up to his feet as long as he's careful about not getting caught in a sub.

Volkov vs. Cortes-Acosta
Waldo Cortes-Acosta, DEC
I see a lot of people on Volkov here but I think Waldo has a style that could present problems to Volkov. Volkov is big, powerful, technically sound, and good at distance management. But he is SLOWWWWW as hell. His movements are slow, his punches are slow, and he's gonna be too slow to get out of the way of Waldos high volume and snappy straight punches. I could see a world where Volkov actually looks to grapple here but I dont have a lot of faith in his takedown offense. I think Salsa Boy cashes as an underdog like he so often does.

Brady vs. Buckley
Sean Brady, DEC
This one is insanely hard to pick not because its a 50/50 but because you just know that whoever loses is getting the doors blown off of them. Im gonna lean Brady here just cause he's more consistent. Sure he's been chinned a couple times but he bounced back pretty well after losing to Belal so I think mentally he's going to be okay getting back in to the cage. He's pretty ****** though if he cant get the takedown. His striking leaves a lot to be desired but again, he only looks bad in these fights where the takedown isnt as big of a threat like with Belal and Morales who are elite wrestlers. Buckley isnt going to put up that kind of resistance which is why I think he gets wrestle ****** and mopped here.

Green vs. Stephens
King Green, TKO, R2
Should be a pretty wild scrap but like.... Why? Why is a  39 year old 145er with 22 losses getting a shot at King Green where there are so many 155ers with name value that could be getting this opportunity?

Błachowicz vs. Guskov
Bogdan Guskov, TKO, R2
Another fight where Im just going... why? Both guys could have moved on to new opponents after that last fight but here we are again running it back. I picked Jan last time but I think Im going Bogdan KO here. He had Jan hurt pretty badly in their last fight and if he didnt get caught himself at the very end of the fight I think he would've won that decision.  Jan is just looking old and stiff for my liking and even though he's probably the most badass 43 year old on the planet right now he's well past his prime and hasnt won a fight in ages.

Alvarez vs. Amosov
Yaroslav Amosov, SUB, R1
Im gonna make the possible mistake of picking this fight based on each guy's last performance. Amosov looked like a beast and he was able to work his grappling on Magny with ease which is actually kind of impressive. Alvarez on the other hand didnt look the same to me at welterweight even with a gimme matchup. Just felt like he looked a bit too skinny and like his power didnt carry over up a weight class which both could be a problem in this one. Give me Youre-A-Slob Im-A-Slob via slobby blobby sub.

posts.author: A_Person_05
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Chimaev vs. Strickland
Sean Strickland, DEC
Honestly I'm picking Strickland more because I want him to win than actually thinking he can win. He has much better striking and cardio than Khamzat, he is a much more well rounded fighter, much more tested. Everything I just said is true about all 3 of Khamzat's last opponents. Khamzat still wins. I just want Strickland to win so the Middleweight division isn't stuck with one title fight every 1.5 years, until the Champ vacates to go to LHW after 2 title defenses.

Volkov vs. Cortes-Acosta
Alexander Volkov, DEC
Volkov via reach, skill, and athleticism. Volkov just pieces him up, similar to the Pavlovich fight for both guys.

Brady vs. Buckley
Sean Brady, DEC
Brady can do exactly what Usman did, maybe even better given where Usman is at in his career. If Buckley wins this by being this insanely physical striker jumping around in out, laterally moving landing big shots, using his insane frame and strength to prevent takedowns, I am going to be so pissed. Because Buckley straight up looked like he was not trying against Usman, if he won that he's getting a title shot rn. And the way Buckley beats Brady if it happens, is the same way he was supposed to beat Usman.

Green vs. Stephens
King Green, DEC
What a fun matchup of the uncs. I think Green is so much faster at where these guys both are, I think he gets it done by decision, using that speed, reach, weird frame. This will be an amazing boxing fight though.

Błachowicz vs. Guskov
Jan Błachowicz, DEC
I picked Jan the first time I'm gonna do it again. He is so savvy. He can win this in more ways. Grappling, kickboxing, leg kick spamming, he has better defense than Guskov, he is more well rounded than Guskov. Guskov just has stupid nuclear power in his hands. He hits people and they look like they don't know what hit them. Guskov hurt Jan worse than Pereira ever did. I just have to pick the dude with more ways to win.

Gautier vs. Diaz
Ateba Gautier, TKO, R1
Gautier murders a can, gets given another can next. Guy will break Anderson's winstreak record before he fights a top 5 opponent. In all seriousness, Gautier will pressure Diaz, Diaz is gonna land some BS like jabs and tappy leg kicks, Gautier will throw some monster combinaion, Diaz is gonna look shocked by the power of the shots because that's what happens when Gautier hits people, and then Gautier is gonna grab the head, and get a knockout with a knee.

Kopylov vs. Tulio
Roman Kopylov, DEC
Kopylov works the body wins a decision. Tulio pressuring Kopylov isn't gonna work out well for him IMO.

Carpenter vs. Ochoa
Jose Ochoa, TKO, R2
Ochoa is damn good IMO. The Almabaev fight was a bad matchup on short notice. He looked damn good against Kavanaugh, hurt Loneer worse than anyone other than Charles Johnson ever has. He looked sick against Durden. He has a big frame, he has big power, he has nasty BJJ, and he has really nice movement skills.

Susurkaev vs. Santos
Baisangur Susurkaev, TKO, R1
Susurkaev is probably the worst undefeated fighter on the roster rn, outside of random contender series NPCs that nobody cares about. So the worst relevant undefeated fighter. His boxing defense is some of the worst in not only that division, but maybe the entire UFC. He does not cover up, he does not move his head, he doesn't even tuck his chin. He is also a tiny middleweight. What he does have going for him is the fact that offensively he seems to have a massive variety of weapons, and has the fight iq to know how to set up those weapons really really well. He will chain together attacks to land big shots, he uses his footwork to land big shots, he knows how to land big leg kicks whenever his opponent is putting weight on their leg, and everything he lands has big power. I don't think Santos is the guy to beat him. He beat Ozzy Diaz, that was a robbery, he should be 2-0 in the UFC, but I think to beat Susurkaev you need to be a finisher. You need to capitalize on his terrible defense, and the fact that he seems small out there. Susurkaev can get Santos into a position to be finished, and Susurkaev is a finisher. If Susurkaev fought like Bruno Ferreira or Gregory Rodrigues, or Paulo Costa, Joe Pyfer, or Bo Nickal, every single one of those guys finish Susurkaev like it's nothing. Santos is not on that level.

Alvarez vs. Amosov
Joel Alvarez, TKO, R2
Alvarez is a dog. Finisher from any position. Power in everything. Finished Luque don't let the result and the ref tell you otherwise, that was a TKO victory via a punch to the eye. Amosov is good but Alvarez is on another level. Losing to Arman doesn't mean anything, getting outgrappled by Arman doesn't mean anyone can outgrapple you, it means you got outgrappled by probably the third best grappler in the UFC.

Dawson vs. Rębecki
Mateusz Rębecki, TKO, R2
Dawson has no chin. He just cannot take a shot. Rebecki has good grappling, is a short guy so he can use that to help defend takedowns, and he just will land a big shot at some point, and Dawson cannot get hit by hard shots.

Also let it be known that this is a ******* banger of a card. So many good fighters or prospects to look out for on this card. It does look like the main card might be a bit of a decision fest, but the prelims is gonna be 95% finishes.

I think we've seen Taira struggle more with strikers than we have seen Van struggle with grapplers and Im not gonna overthink it here. Yes Taira is bigger, yes he's more well rounded and yes he's been training MMA for longer but Im telling you I have a funny feeling Van stuffs the takedowns and pieces him up over the course of 5 rounds.

Gautier vs. Diaz
Ateba Gautier, TKO, R1
Lol this is so stupid. Susarkaev vs Gautier was right there but I guess we need to see these guys both on 5 fight win streaks before ever facing anyone decent.

Kopylov vs. Tulio
Marco Tulio, DEC
Another well matched fight but Im taking Tulio to bounce back. Kopylov is a bit overrated and doesnt handle pressure fighters very well as we've seen in his last two fights. Tulio is a bit defensively irresponsible but I think he doesnt have to worry about a flash KO like he did last fight which will lead to a better performance.

Carpenter vs. Ochoa
Jose Ochoa, DEC
Good match making here. Both guys have a lot of potential but have fallen a bit short of the hype. I think I like Ochoa's upside a bit more so Im going to lean him to win here. Kind of a 50/50 though so if I can get Carpenter at decent underdog money I might take a swing.

Susurkaev vs. Santos
Baisangur Susurkaev, TKO, R2
I hate this guy and hope he loses but I cant see Santos doing more than just taking a round off him early in the fight with volume. I think eventually the body shots from Susarkaev will make him crumble to the mat.

Dawson vs. Rębecki
Mateusz Rębecki, TKO, R1
Havent really been on the Rebecki side of things as of late but in this matchup I think hes got a really strong chance to get back in the win column. His striking and grappling are both really solid its just that the guy cant seem to avoid getting his face rearranged every time he's out there. Dawson has a similar problem with getting hit only difference is that when he gets hit clean he gets finished. I think because of that Rebecki has a good chance to put him away via TKO.

Sabatini vs. Gomis
Pat Sabatini, DEC
Kind of messed up that UFC is giving Sabatini a step down in competition but its not totally shocking. They arent going to reward guys with boring fighting styles and zero fan fair with big matchups and I totally agree with it. From what Ive seen this fight can be tricky considering Gomis is a good striker with really good scrambles and takedown defense against the cage. Only problem is Pat Sabatini is like a Merab where he'll just keep shooting takedowns and failing until he finally gets it. The only formula to beat him that we've seen so far is to put his lights out but Gomis isnt a very powerful guy so I think this will be a victory grinded out by Pat in the later rounds.

posts.author: JeanSilvaEnjoyer
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Chimaev vs. Strickland
Chimaev, DEC
No one stops Chimaev from taking them down, and I don't think Sean will be any different. The main question I have is whether Chimaev will find a finish. I hope he is a little more aggressive here than he was against Dricus because that fight was a pain to watch, and Sean seems to be doing everything possible to make Chimaev extremely pissed off. Maybe that's part of the gameplan. Maybe he's trying to make Chimaev angry and more reckless. I don't think it'll make any difference though. Sean's a tough guy to KO and a tough guy to submit, so I'll predict he makes it to the final bell.

Van vs. Taira
Taira, DEC
Van is clearly the superior striker here, and Taira is clearly the superior grappler. I like both guys, and I'd like to see Van stay champ for a while, but I'm a little worried for the guy. For one thing, he's coming off some sort of minor injury that caused them to push the fight back a couple of weeks. It might mean nothing, or it might mean a week or two where he could not train. The best evidence we have for Van's grappling defense is the Tsuruya fight. And while Tsuruya's decent and Van did a good job of staying on his feet, Tsuruya is nowhere near Taira's level. Not even close. Of course, that was over a year ago, and Van continuously shows that he is growing at a rapid pace. But Taira is young, too, and I'm not so sure who's growing more in the shorter period of time. Van hits hard. He's KO'd Bunes and Silva, and he was damn close to KO'ing Durden and Royval, all of which he managed to do in 3 rounds. The thing that pushes me away from picking him is how Tsuruya's takedown attempts kept Van from putting out the same volume that he normally does. While Van still won that fight 30-27, Tsuruya really wasn't able to rack up any meaningful amount of control time, and I think Taira will. He could find a submission, but Van's only ever been subbed once in his early career, so I think that's unlikely. I find it more likely that we go to a 48-47 decision where 50% of people call it a robbery afterward, regardless of who wins.

Volkov vs. Cortes-Acosta
Volkov, DEC
Waldo has been looking great recently and I'm really tempted to go with him here, but Volkov is a mainstay of this division. I'm a little concerned because Volkov is 37, and he didn't look great against Almeida even if he did technically win. The thing that gets me about Waldo is that yes, he's coming off three KO wins, but the quality of competition is a little suspicious. Gaziev is a decent HW despite the memes, but he's not ever going to be in title contention. I thought more of Delija at the time, but the loss to Spivac he took (a loss in a primarily striking-based fight, mind you) is making me reevaluate my position on Delija. And Derrick Lewis is done. He can either chin you in the first round of a fight, or else you win. I still can't get the memory of Waldo almost losing to Spivac out of my mind, or him smudging Despaigne to a decision. I might switch on this one, but I'm leaning Volkov just because I tend to doubt the guy before each fight, and then he seems to always make me wonder why I doubted him.

Brady vs. Buckley
Buckley, TKO, R2
100% biased pick, but idgaf. WE are one of the most entertaining contenders at 170, WE are going to slime out Sean Brady, and then WE are going to get slimed out by Michael Morales or Carlos Prates. I understand why Brady's the favorite here because Joaquin fought Usman like he had never seen a takedown before in his life. It was a terrible look and a humiliating loss. I didn't know this before the Usman fight, but one of the announcers said that Buckley doesn't train with the best competition in the gym, and after looking at the page for "Finney's HIT Squad," yeah, that seems to be the case. But Buckley's been talking big on social media about how he's becoming "Buckgomedov" and he's never going to be held down by another man again. Of course, that's all talk, and who knows how much work he's actually doing on takedown defense in the gym. It's so easy to imagine Brady doing the exact same thing that Usman did to Buckley (and maybe even finding the sub), but I'm skeptical. Most of Brady's fight with Burns played out with striking in the center minus a couple of takedowns and a lot of clinching against the cage. I think he's gotten just a bit lucky with his matchups given that one of his last five was against Burns on his way out the door, and two of the others were against Edwards and Gastelum, who I don't think take their careers seriously anymore. Now, the exact same criticism could be made of Buckley because he beat ultra-washed Colby Covington and 41 year old Wonderboy. But I think Buckley is dangerous on the feet, Brady's chin is not the best, and I'm hoping that Buckley's not lying about training to defend those takedowns.

Green vs. Stephens
Green, DEC
I'm thinking it's probably not a finish because Stephens is generally a pretty durable guy. I mean, he just went five rounds in BKFC with Mike Perry before getting finished, and that means he can take a punch. But Bobby Green has looked good recently, and I don't think Stephens is ready for UFC competition again.

Gautier vs. Diaz
Gautier, TKO, R1
I mean, I like watching Ateba knock these guys out as much as the next guy, but this matchmaking by the UFC is really slimy. They'll give PFL guys someone ranked in their first or second bout, but they're protecting Ateba like he's a precious jewel.

Alvarez vs. Amosov
Amosov, DEC
I've got a sneaking suspicion that Alvarez did well at 155 because of his physical frame and because he could out grapple most of his opponents. Those things are both gone for him here. Although Alvarez handled business against Luque, it wasn't quite the statement win that I expected. I'm still not sold on this guy at 170, but maybe I'm an idiot. Amosov faced a pretty strong level of competition in Bellator, but at the end of the day, it's still Bellator.

Dawson vs. Rebecki
Rebecki, TKO, R2
Round-betski has been through a lot of wars and has built up a lot of scar tissue, so if he gets flat backed by Dawson and starts eating elbows, I expect to see a finish similar to the one Dawson got over Rafa Garcia. But I feel like I haven't seen Rebecki on his back a whole lot in the UFC. The only times I remember were against CDF after he was already badly hurt and against Orolbai. He was able to withstand Orolbai's grappling quite well considering what we've seen Orolbai do to guys like Musayev and Chris Curtis, but maybe that was because he and Orolbai were covered in blood for most of that fight. I think Dawson is going to have a lot of trouble getting Rebecki to the mat. Rebecki's built like a bulldog. And even though we've seen Rebecki outworked on the feet by CDF, Ludovit Klein, and Chris Duncan, I don't think Dawson's on that level in terms of his striking. One thing that gives me concern is that Dawson is super close with Chris Duncan, so Duncan will have plenty of pointers to give to his teammate going into this bout. Either way, Rebecki makes everyone who fights him go through hell to win, and my early lean is that Dawson won't get his takedowns and will not be able to hang with Rebecki on the feet.

Miller vs. Gordon
Gordon, DEC
Jared Gordon nearly beat Rafa Garcia with one leg after a car crash. In his prime, I'd pick Jim Miller to beat Gordon, but this ain't the Golden Era anymore.

Kopylov vs. Tulio
Tulio, DEC
Not really sure on this one. Kopylov's not looked great recently, but he's lost against guys who Tulio would also lose to. Meanwhile, Tulio got absolutely demolished by CLD in a way that I find it hard to believe Kopylov would get demolished. Might switch up on this one. It's mostly vibes.

Sabatini vs. Gomis
Sabatini, DEC
I think Chepe Mariscal is a great fighter, and I remember him cutting Sabatini open badly early in their fight. That's a nightmare for Sabatini especially because the blood is an extra obstacle to his grappling, but he got that fight where he wanted it and secured the W. Gomis, meanwhile, is also a talented dude, but I don't like how he makes everything so, so close. Split decisions with Marshall. Split decision with Brito. Close decision with Ruchala. Sabatini is kind of past his prime here, but I reckon he wins. Probably by split decision.

Susurkaev vs. Santos
Susurkaev, TKO, R3
Low-key a closer fight than the pick percentage would have you believe. Maybe I'm wrong about Susurkaev, but he seems a little over hyped just because he's Khamzat's training partner. He's looked pretty good in the UFC so far, and I don't think he's a scrub by any means, but if he wants to be a real contender at 185, he shouldn't be losing rounds to Eric Nolan and Eric McConico. He plays around and gets much too ****y in the cage. Even though Djorden Santos is a goofy ahh fighter who will almost certainly lose here, he does not give up, and I expect him to give Susurkaev problems early.

Carpenter vs. Ochoa
Ochoa, TKO, R2
The UFC is arguably giving Clayton his toughest fight yet even though he's on a two-fight losing streak. I think Ochoa is destined for the top 10. He got Lone'er Kavanagh on his debut and took Kavanagh to a super close decision. He blew through the GOAT Cody "Custom Made" Durden, and then he did pretty well pulling up on super-short notice against Asu Almabayev, who is a stylistic nightmare for Ochoa. I don't think Carpenter is going to be able to keep Ochoa down, and I think he's going to get hurt badly on the feet. Decision's probably the safer pick, but I'm going with my faith in big Jose here.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Miller vs Gordon
Gordon KO RD 3

Awesome Unc fight right here, these are the types of fights I want to see Miller in. I must admit I was not expecting him to show up here at all; I know he’s still kicking but this could genuinely be his retirement fight. Gordon is from Astoria as well, so this is a scrap between two locals. The line is a little wide to me for a Gordon fight; he’s a great OG but imo has always been a little overrated because of the Paddy fight. I expect him to win this fight very easily and maybe even find a finish, but -350 on a guy who’s also old, near the end of his career, fragile, and has awful luck is never the play. As gross as this line is though, I don’t really see a PTV for Miller outside of Gordon’s durability failing him again. Miller is an OG and it’s been great seeing him play the role of gatekeeper but he is washed as ****. He runs through the cans and equally washed vets but when he fights someone competent, Miller gets the floor wiped with him. He’s in very good form for 42 and still has his skills for the most part, but he is oooooooold. MIller has power early but he is slow, clunky, and hittable. He hasn’t gotten ko’ed since the Hooker fight but he has been dropped a few times on this streak and taken a ton of damage. I was impressed when he fought Benitez because you could see Miller adapting his game to the times (lots of leg kicking there), but he’s still pretty slow. Gordon’s main flaw on the feet is his awful chin, but I don’t see Miller being able to do much of anything to him on the feet. He is always live to catch Gordon early but outside of that, I think Gordon styles on him. His hand speed is going to be hilariously faster here, his defense much sharper (Gordon actually very hard to hit clean despite his terrible chin), and just overall I think he’s a much better striker. Miller still has power and his striking is good, but I just think Gordon is going to be very hard for him to outwork. Gordon’s last fight was a bad look but it’s aging really well, especially considering how much success he had despite one leg. Watching that one back, he was literally teeing off on Rafa in r2 and was hurting him so bad he was freezing in place. Gordon isn’t much of a ko guy but recently he’s weirdly looked quite powerful. Like I said, he hurt Rafa multiple times, he completely stiffed Moises/Madsen (the former somehow nobody has been able to KO like that), and he was giving Haqparast hell. I think he’s a better striker than Miller anyways but given Miller’s cardio issues, I think he is ****** here outside of a r1 ko. Cardio is one of Gordon’s best attributes and he uses it as a weapon all the time. He can keep a very high pace, which Miller cannot. Miller hasn’t won a decision since 2016 and in current form has about 7.5 minutes of gas. He slows a lot quicker when grappled but I don’t see Gordon shooting on him. Miller is still veyr durable but at 42 and with how much Gordon throws, I kind of see him getting ko’ed here. I know that sounds square but idk, Gordon has been looking weirdly very powerful lately, and I hate how much of a punching bag Miller becomes once he tires. Miller also won’t have much success with grappling here either. He can get takedowns but Gordon is difficult to take/hold down, and he’s a pretty legit brown belt himself, so he won’t panic. Miller’s grappling is crafty but he can be reversed and put on his back fairly easily. Again, I don’t see Gordon grappling him but if he survived on the mat with a very legit 4th degree black belt (Leo Santos), I think he will be fine against Miller. Miller has one round to win this fight, if this goes past r1 he is going to get melted here. Maybe I’m getting a little overzealous with KO, but Miller does not wear damage well at all, and Gordon has a massive cardio/pace edge here that is going to cause a lot of problems. I am worried that he may have slowed down from the Rafa fight but even then, he’s facing a dude who’s pushing 60 in fight years and is a couple fights from retirement. I expect a clean performance from Gordon here but I think he can get a finish if he really goes for it; Gordon has been unnaturally aggressive these last 4-5 fights of his career. If he comes out with the pace he did in his last 3 fights, I don’t see Miller making it to the final bell. Not even durability wise, Miller simply doesn’t have the cardio to keep up.

Kopylov vs Tulio
Kopylov KO RD 3

I did a 180 on this one with tape. I’ve never been a big fan of Kopylov and was expecting this to be a fade spot, but now I feel the exact opposite; I like him quite a bit here honestly. It’s funny to say that because I’ve never been a huge fan of his and usually fade him in the majority of his fights. I was originally expecting another fade because of Tulio’s style; Kopylov has always struggled with in-your-face, pressure heavy brawlers like Tulio. However, Tulio just doesn’t do it for me. Sure, he’s exciting and loves a brawl but I don’t think his skills are that amazing. He is a brawler at heart and isn’t afraid to take one to give one. I kind of question his chin as well, he’s been knocked completely out cold in both of his losses and even guys like Gore were hurting him badly when he actually landed strikes. I like his aggression and output but his striking has never wow’d me; when he can’t force a brawl in the pocket he’s always looked very pedestrian to me. Not that he’s a bad striker, but imo there’s nothing overly special about his striking skillset. Trying to brawl with Kopylov is a very dangerous proposition; for all his flaws he is an extremely good striker with an emphasis on kicks, significantly faster limb speed than Tulio, more power, and much more precision as well. Kopylov did get embarrassed in his last two fights but he also fought two top contenders so I’ll cut him some slack; both of those guys have significantly more complete and diverse games than Tulio. Kopylov actually managed to wobble both badly as well, but couldn’t capitalize on it. Costa is now one fight away from the title at 205 and Robocop has always been a top 10 level guy to me, so I don’t fault Kopylov for losing these. Like I said, he managed to hurt both guys and while kind of flat performances, he did much better than I originally thought. Costa and Robocop were also able to cook Kopylov from orthodox and shut down his body kicks; Tulio is orthodox himself but he is almost entirely a boxer. Again, I like his output and aggression but being primarily a boxer, he doesn’t blow me away; I think a lot of his success has been predicated on his physical qualities. He can definitely put hands on Kopylov but the problem with this bruiser style is that Tulio has to take one to give one. This works against bottom feeders like Ihor and Gore, but it will go very poorly if he tries this against Kopylov. Kopylov can be hurt but he’s historically very durable and imo has the best technique of anyone Tulio has fought (CLD is a weird case imo because his whole style is unconventional, Kopylov sticks to kickboxing basics and is very good at them). If Tulio tries to brawl I think Kopylov can come right back with boxing of his own, and he also has his kicks to control the distance. Like I said, Tulio is almost exclusively a boxer, he hasn’t dealt with many kickers at all, and I think Kopylov is really going to punish him with his signature body kick here. It will be open due to Tulio’s stance and all he knows to do is plod forward, so I think he will walk right into it all fight. This is not only one of Kopylov’s best weapons, but it will pay dividends here as Tulio is a gasser. Kopylov has always had very weird cardio issues of his own, but I think he’s somewhat figured them out; his body language is bad but he’s learned to fight through it, and actually r3 has been his best round in his last couple of fights. Tulio’s cardio wasn’t an issue to me before but he redlined hard against CLD so it did raise some questions; CLD wasn’t even working the body either, Tulio just kind of broke down. Overall this fight is going to be 95% striking, and I have to favor Kopylov in that scenario. IMO, he has a much better skillset/process on the feet, more power, and is more proven against real comp. Tulio is a fun brawler but he is predominantly a boxer, is very hittable, has a suspect chin/cardio, and while he’s still young, at 31 years of age I tend to believe what we see now is the best Tulio we’re going to get. I've always been a Kopylov hater but I think he has established himself as a solid gatekeeper to the top 15, and I do not think Tulio is that. You also have to factor in that this is a pretty rough “bounce back” fight from your first loss, this is not an easy layup whatsoever.

Sabatini vs Gomis
Sabatini SUB RD 2

Kind of a weird fight, I get both are outside the top 15 but this is just a weird style clash and also seems strange from a momentum standpoint too. I like both of these guys, I never pick against either of them because they know exactly what they’re good at, never deviate, and are extremely consistent. They do have their fair share of flaws but the pros outweigh the cons, and I love backing both as underdogs. The line seems a little wide to me here given how tricky Gomis is/how bad Sabatini’s chin is, but at the same time I kind of feel like Sabatini will show levels here. Gomis has pretty good anti grappling but I don’t think his tdd/defensive grappling is on a similar level to Sabatini’s. In fact, he hasn’t faced a grappler close to this level and has honestly shown some bad looks on his back. It is very difficult to hold him down but if you can get him there he is out of sorts. Marshall came very close to subbing him in r3, I think he won but he had a very hard time scraping by Brito, and Ruchala was able to have a lot of success taking him down. I think Sabatini is easily the best grappler Gomis has fought in his career, and he has the wrestling to match; that’s one of the main reasons I love backing Pat, not only is he an elite grappler but he can wrestle his ass off as well. He has great cage wrestling like all the Philly guys, and he can wrestle in the open. He also has experience in Sambo to boot, so he really covers all the angles. He is strong as **** and has grounded everyone he’s fought (save for the KO losses), so I don’t see why he can’t score tds here. Gomis will show resistance but I think he will struggle when Sabatini starts to re-shoot. Once on bottom, I think the round is over, if not the fight. Gomis has shown struggles to get to his feet in the past and again, this is easily the best wrestler he’s faced so far. On the feet you obviously have to favor Gomis, but I think his style will work against him here. Gomis is a great striker with insanely crafty defense but he is a point fighter through and through. He has almost no ko power at all and never looks to chase a finish either. He loves to fight on the backfoot and is perfectly fine to let his opponents advance. He is obviously a better striker here but if he’s not able to hurt Pat I kind of think he’s ****** here. Sure, he can win minutes on the feet but he needs to do damage or keep Pat from shooting because I don’t see him being able to stop the wrestling attack for 15 minutes. Sabatini has a glass jaw and no striking so I wouldn’t be entirely shocked if he gets KO’ed, but it’s very unlikely imo. Once he’s able to ground Gomis I think he should dominate. Gomis’s game off of his back is pretty bad and we’ve seen the grapplers he’s fought in the past cause a ton of issues once they can ground him. Sabatini is without a doubt the best wrestler/grappler Gomis has fought, and I think his risk-adverse, patient style is going to be the death of him here. He needs to show Sabatini no respect if he wants to win; running around and point fighting is just going to give him more opportunities to shoot, and won’t put him in any danger. Gomis is a master of point fighitng but fighting a perfect fight against a top level grappler like Sabatini is a very dangerous proposition imo. The line seems a little wide but I feel like Sabatini is going to get him down at some point and just maul him from there. I think he will find a finish even though Gomis is durable.

Susurkaev vs Santos
Susurkaev DEC

I really want to take Susurkaev by finish, especially since I think Santos is overrated (in terms of his success in the ufc) but I also don’t want to be triggered when he drags Santos along. I know a lot of people think Susurkaev is a fraud but I disagree. Is he as good as his hype train currently is? No, but I think the talent is clearly there, he’s just green and has the ****iness of an undefeated guy. His nonchalant style has gotten him in a good bit of trouble, and while I agree with most that struggling with these guys is not a great look but at the same time, I think Susurkaev is ******* around. Imo it’s not due to a lack of skill, he just knows he’s better than these guys. For what it’s worth, the McConico win is aging well and I also think Eric Nolan is significantly better than his record shows (he will actually win a good amount of fights imo). This is a good test for him because he is going to lose if he decides to show up like his last two fights. I really dislike Santos, I think the only thing he’s good at is taking a beating. He forces a ******** pace in every fight but I don’t think his cardio is that crazy. He fights through it well but Santos usually drowns with his opponents and fights ahead because he has a will to win. Santos is also on all the EPO; if you watch his regional fights he is gassing after 5 minutes and trash at everything, but now that he’s at ATT he fights at a Holloway pace every fight. He lands an insane amount of strikes but has no power at all; he’s just a ****roach. He kind of reminds me of a worse Danny Silva; he never gets out of your face and is extremely durable. Susurkaev is a significantly better striker than him imo, it just all depends on how he wants to approach this fight. If he wants to taunt all fight he is going to get swamped but if he locks in and fights behind his reach/uses his striking skillset I think he should easily pick Santos apart. Just last fight I thought Barlow was easily outworking him at range, but threw the fight away once he rocked Santos and chased a finish. Santos swamped him because he tanked the shots and doubled his output. I thought he beat Diaz pretty clearly but Santos “lost” that one because he had no power to stop Diaz’s volume and was just a punching bag, drawing awful reactions any time he gets hit. Maybe this is close and grindy because Susurkaev likes to **** around, but I think his skills are way above Santos’s. He has also shown that he knows how to wrestle, and his cardio seems fine as well. I expect him to slow if Santos enters brawler mode but even then I think he can mix in takedowns and cruise here. I want to take KO or maybe even a sneaky sub (I expect Susurkaev to grapple), but his finishing ability is so bad. He reminds me of Navajo a little in that regard; clear talent but awful urgency in the cage. This should be a good fight to learn more about him since Santos is durable and very annoying to fight.

Carpenter vs Ochoa
Ochoa KO RD 2

This is such a banger to kick the card off. The one caveat of this fight is that this may sadly be loser goes home; this is presumably the last fight on Ochoa’s contract (unless he got a new one for the Asu fight), and if Carpenter loses he is definitely gone with his L streak. It kind of sucks because I think both of these guys are better than their records show; the UFC needlessly pushed both too soon. That being said, I kind of think this is a nightmare matchup for Carpenter. He does have the grappling advantage but everywhere else I feel like he is just a step behind. I really like Carpenter due to his well rounded background, but imo that’s a tad bit overrated; he is well rounded but outside of BJJ I wouldn’t say he’s incredible in any one area. He can push a pace, strike, and grapple but as we’ve seen the more skilled guys in the UFC has given him issues. Ochoa on the other hand is the typical Chute Boxe style we all know and love: massive weight bully with an insane brawler style and never ending cardio, but terrible tdd. Ochoa has gotten outclassed in both of his losses but his level of comp has been pretty silly thus far; he never should’ve taken the Asu fight either, it killed all his momentum. That fight is not the greatest look because it showed us that Ochoa’s tdd is not good. That was a red flag initially to me because Carpenter’s strength is bjj, but I think Ochoa’s get ups are enough, as well as being extremely physical for the weight class. He is very reckless and can be taken down fairly easily which I don’t like, but I did like to see his get ups against Asu. Asu is one of the best pure wrestlers in the division so I don’t fault him a ton for that, but while fresh Ochoa was getting to his feet well, and even hit a back take of his own. Carpenter has good bjj but he can be reversed way too easily and his control isn’t that amazing either. The only guys he’s dominated on the mat at the ufc level have nonexistent ground games; Ochoa is a bad wrestler but he can grapple and isn’t content to sit in guard the entire time. I think Carpenter is a little outmatched physically as well, as we saw in his last two losses and dwcs; I think this is a big problem here because Ochoa is massive for the division and juiced to the gills. I expect there to be some bad moments but I think Ochoa can stay safe and get to his feet. If this stays standing I think he steamrolls Carpenter. Ochoa does get hit a good amount with his style but he is also that classic Chute Boxe brawler: nonstop pressure, iron chin, unlimited cardio, great muay thai. I love his front kicks to the body and he has hurt multiple people with them. Carpenter is a serviceable striker but I don’t like his defense at all. He fights with his chin high and while he can scrap, he becomes pretty hittable in extended sequences (see his dwcs fight for example). Carpenter also got wobbled pretty badly before getting subbed against Filho, which I did not remember the first time around. Carpenter has a PTV with takedowns here, but I worry that will also be what kills him here. I have a feeling he is going to be looking for a td from bell to bell, and without confidence on the feet I think Ochoa will continuously get up and melt Carpenter/Carpenter gasses himself out trying to control Ochoa over and over. Lastly there are some intangibles that worry me on the Carpenter side; he said he was taking some time away from MMA but this is a decently quick turnaround, and at 29 I worry we have seen him peak early; especially when you consider his lengthy amateur combat sports career.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Gautier vs Diaz
Gautier KO RD 1

I really don’t get why the UFC felt the need to rebook this but cool I guess. We won’t learn anything new about Ateba but his last fight showed he can use the experience, so I’m okay with it. You can tell the UFC is in love with him as well, not only is he getting easy fights but he’s getting high placement on big PPV cards. His last fight was disappointing but not the worst look ever. It showed that he needs to improve his process beyond hook spam, and that he gets ploddy with his pressure. It is worth noting that Pulyaev is better than he seems, but it was still pretty underwhelming. Gautier won the fight easily and actually showed me some positives in terms of cardio and carrying power late, but he still has a long way to go. That's not much of an issue though, as he’s still insanely young and there are plenty of cans to build him up with. The reason I don’t like this fight though is that we won’t learn anything new here. I expect an easy r1 KO, and this honestly is a step down from Pulyaev imo. Diaz “won” his last fight but it was a robbery, either way going life and death with a fat WW who has no power is an awful look. The only good thing I can say about Ozzy is that he is well conditioned; pretty much all of his wins are from him taking a beating early and then coming back on a death gassed opponent. He’s a big guy being a former LHW but his overall skills are not too impressive. He’s not overly dangerous either despite the finish rate; he was landing at will on Santos and his strikes seemed to have no effect whatsoever. He’s not going to test the wrestling and will brawl with Ateba, so I think he’s ******. We still have questions surrounding Ateba’s tdd and ability to take a punch, but I kind of doubt either get tested here. I guess I could maybe see Diaz tiring him out, but that would require him surviving the early onslaught. I didn’t like how Ateba was throwing the same combo repeatedly last fight; I think that speaks to his inexperience. He can probably KO Ozzy with that same combo but I would like to see him show something else besides hooks. I expect a brutal r1 KO here though. UFC knows what they’re doing putting this fight as the featured prelims; they want Ateba to catch a body to amp people up before the main card.

Alvarez vs Amosov
Amosov DEC

I really hope Joel doesn’t have visa issues, this is a very sick fight; been patiently awaiting news for both of these guys. I’m not really sure what to think of Joel at 170. He’s still going to be massive but given his style I'm a little unsure what will happen when he faces good grapplers (like Amosov). The Luque fight was completely bizarre as well, so that’s not really a great gauge either. Joel dominated every second of the fight but it was a weird showing from both. He ko’ed Luque in r1 with a punch to the eye but the corrupt Brazilian commission counted it as a poke, and then Luque retired on the stool after a beatdown in r2 but his corner forced him to come back out. I want to say Joel felt bad and that’s why he just laid on top in all of r3 but that was really weird to see from both guys; I know Luque quits when you show him the door but Joel seemed weirdly very tired and just laid on top until the fight ended. Now, this was short notice (something Joel has never done) and his 170 debut against a guy he basically finished twice, so I will cut him some slack, but it was a weird fight overall. You also have to factor in that Joel is 33 and one of the most inactive guys on the roster, so it’s possible that time away caught up to him. I have always been a huge fan of Joel’s game but I think this is a really bad matchup for him. Amosov is a super legit grappler and Joel has not shown me improvements in his wrestling over time. I will say, he actually showed some form of defense in the Brener/Diakese/Luque fights but he can still be taken down far too easily and has plenty of bad habits on the mat. His bjj and guard game is phenomenal, but you can only go so far with work from your back. Yaroslav is obviously not Arman but he’s the only good wrestler Joel has faced and dominated him from pillar to post. If you watch Yaroslav’s Bellator fights you’ll see his control is nothing special, but that has to do with matchups. Yaroslav had so many hard matchups in Bellator, 90% of his opponents wrestled d1 or had some sort of accomplishment in wrestling. He wasn’t able to lay on these guys but you also have to factor in that he has been facing very high level wrestlers for the most part. Joel is not going to show resistance to takedowns like that, and I actually think Yaroslav will get free takedowns here. He has shown he can fight at a super high level pace with great scrambling. Yaroslav is far too good of a grappler to get caught lacking in Joel’s guard imo. It can happen, but very unlikely imo; he’d have to take a bad shot and give his neck up. Like I said, Yaroslav didn’t show super dominant control in Bellator, but he was also facing guys who don’t accept being on bottom and were credentialed wrestlers; Joel not only accepts bottom position but he has no wrestling background at all and a tdd in the 30% range. Joel is a dangerous submission threat but not an amazing grappler imo. What I mean by this is we never see Joel do anything other than guard play. He never looks to hold top control (besides last fight but that was an outlier imo), he never looks to swing to the back, never looks for reversals, etc. He just wants to play guard and fish for a triangle/armbar. He’s hit one reversal in his entire ufc career, and that came after getting easily outwrestled for 4.5 minutes against Belluardo. Seeing the scrambling in the Storely fight leads me to believe that Yaroslav can survive bad spots on the ground and rack up control here. I really think tds will come with little to no resistance, and Yaroslav is a very legit grappler. This is also the first grappler Joel has faced at 170, and I have a feeling his already bad tdd will not hold up well against bigger guys. Like always, Joel is still the bigger guy but Yaroslav is not small whatsoever. I don’t see this staying standing for long but if it does, I have to favor Joel. His striking is nasty and he uses his reach well. He’s also super fast for his size and can throw all sorts of dynamic strikes. Yaroslav’s striking is serviceable but he doesn’t like getting hit and does panic a little bit at times. The Jackson fight is obviously going to haunt him for the rest of his life, but he looked timid standing against Millender as well. He can throw strikes and like I said, he is serviceable but in extended striking exchanges I favor Joel by a solid margin. One thing I’ve never liked about Joel though is that he’s very hittable and heavily relies on tall man’s defense to slip shots. I don’t think Yaroslav will touch him but at 170 I don’t think Joel can get away with the same things. This may end up being kind of boring just based on styles, but I think Yaroslav should put on a grappling masterclass here. I love Joel’s sub game and he is an amazing finisher but he has a lot of bad habits on the mat that he’s been able to get away with because he’s been facing bad grapplers/fighters for the most part. At 170 against a very legit grappler who’s proven to have good cardio and wrestling, I think Joel is in some trouble here. He’s a good grappler so I think decision, but if Yaroslav is getting 4+ minutes of control with no resistance, I think he will lock up his signature d’arce choke at some point. Again, Yaroslav is no Arman but seeing how easily he shut down Joel’s game with top control has me very worried against a legit wrestler/grappler who is comparable in size. Joel hasn’t shown massive improvements since then, nor has he been active or proving himself against good comp either; he’s looked amazing but has had 4 complete layups in a row, and still shown the same flaws in his striking/tdd.

Dawson vs Rebecki
Rebecki KO RD 1

This is one of the hardest fights on the entire card for me to call; I watched a lot of tape and still don’t really have a good read on this one. I want to say this line is massive recency bias; Rebecki has clearly shown he’s not better than top 25-30, and is arguably getting his toughest fight yet, yet he's the tap fav and lined closely against a much more proven guy. At the same time though, Dawson has clearly hit his ceiling and proven that he is td or bust. It’s TBD how good/bad that Torres loss will age but Dawson looked like pure **** there. He was shooting telegraphed takedowns from a mile away with no setup, and looked petrified on the feet. I was in the arena for that fight and was so baffled by the gameplan there; it was like he knew he stood no chance and just accepted his L. In hindsight that was always going to be a hard matchup but still, it was alarming how he had literally 0 answers to get the fight to the ground. We already knew that Dawson was td or bust but that fight kind of exposed him imo; now everyone he fights is going to bum rush him on the feet because it’s clear as day he has no chin and no form of striking either; all three losses coming by r1 ko is not a coincidence. Dawson is an elite wrestler/grappler and I do like backing him because you know what you’re getting. This is such a tricky fight though. I want to say he can repeatedly take Rebecki down and rinse him, but Rebecki is a capable grappler himself and a wayyy better striker. I taped pretty much Rebecki’s entire UFC career and sadly there is just not a good gauge of him facing good grapplers. I’ve seen him taken down but it’s hard to gauge because he’s only faced like 10 attempts. CDF and Orolbai were able to hold him down as the fight got extended, but that was also due to Rebecki redlining and having nothing left; while fresh he pops up to his feet instantly. Rebecki is a very tiny LW, which normally I dislike but here it may help in terms of defense. We know Rebecki is going to pressure from the jump which Grant already hates, but I think his frame and pressure will stop the tds from coming. Obviously Grant will shoot like his life depends on it, but I weirdly think it may be difficult to get him down while fresh. Rebecki’s tdd didn’t amaze me but he is a muscle hamster with a low center of gravity, so it’s actually kind of hard to get to the hips on him (probably why most don’t shoot on him). I expect Dawson to get takedowns but he may have to work for it. Pressure also makes it hard to score takedowns since Dawson will be going backwards/it makes his shots more telegraphed than they already are. Then you add in the nonstop chaos that Rebecki brings and I kind of think Dawson is going to have a long night here. Rebecki’s striking is not very good and he is walking CTE, but that’s pretty much irrelevant here; Dawson is the worst striker Rebecki has fought in his entire career and is absolutely zero threat on the feet, so Rebecki won’t have to worry about falling behind on damage/scar tissue opening up. If Rebecki can get the fight where he wants I honestly think he will look -500; it’s just clear at this point Dawson has no plan B if he can’t get takedowns, and he lowkey just quit to Torres as well. I normally hate backing Rebecki on the feet but I really don’t see him struggling here. He will attack the legs a lot which is something Dawson has never really had to deal with, he is the far superior boxer, and he has the pressure to make Dawson uncomfortable. Rebecki is a good grappler himself, so I think he can hang on the mat. Obviously if taken down I expect Dawson to swamp him, but I think Rebecki can get to his feet and while fresh, avoid getting owned from bottom. This is a pretty binary fight and I think whoever gets the fight where they want will dominate, it’s just very hard to pick who wins. I think Dawson is a better fighter than Rebecki, and this is also yet another hard fight for Rebecki, but at the same time if he can stop the initial tds there is a very good chance he just breaks Dawson early. Not sure where to go with this one, I lean Rebecki for now but I’m worried this could be a levels fight where Dawson shows superior wrestling yet again

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Chimaev vs Strickland
Chimaev SUB RD 1

I don’t really think this fight is going to be competitive. Sean looked great last fight but this is a whole different monster. Sean does have good tdd and defensive grappling but nothing he’s done tells me he can stop the wrestling attack. Chimaev’s last fight wasn’t the most exciting but he showed us he can wrestle for the entire fight if need be. Strickland hasn’t actually faced a ton of guys who are constantly spamming takedowns on him; Fluffy would’ve been a good gauge for that but Sean shut his game down. DDP in the first fight is the only guy who’s wrestled him recently and he took Sean down with little effort. He wasn’t able to hold him down for very long but the fact he struggled a lot with the initial attempts is not the best sign. I was originally thinking Sean will survive early or at least not get finished but I'm honestly not sure. Chimaev is so fast and moves so well on the ground, I think he will be a step ahead of Sean the entire time. Sean has also been spewing a lot of hatred towards Chimaev and insulting his religion, which I’m sure pisses him off a lot; I don’t usually factor in outside stuff but if Chimaev gets him down early like he does everyone, I don’t think he is going to just ride out control and sniff the entire fight. I’m also not sure Sean is going to look great against an elite opponent; he looked great last fight but imo that was an easy win for him. Fluffy’s wrestling and control was overrated and he is a very bad nail. Strickland is a great gatekeeper but he has always struggled with the elites of the division, usually getting smoked as well. Sean also looked awful in the DDP rematch, so im not sure he can take a beatdown like that again. I don’t think he can stop the takedowns but even if this does remain on the feet, Chimaev can stand and bang with Strickland. I think his cardio issues have always been very overrated and he fights smarter now as well. Strickland has a good pace but his issue has always been that a lot of his volume is insignificant; all he does is jab and teep. He’s a great striker but at the elite level I don’t think it works that well. Chimaev is hittable but he is a good striker and hits hard as well. These two have also sparred multiple rounds together; Sean loves to talk about it but he never mentions that Chimaev beat his ass. I actually do like Sean and I picked him to win his last fight, but he is always someone who will struggle against elite fighters. Love or hate Chimaev, he is an insane talent and Strickland of all people is definitely not the one to beat him

Van vs Taira
Van KO RD 4

This is one of the best title fights we’ve had all year, we finally get to see the new guard at flyweight. I’m a big fan of both of these guys and it’s great we finally get to see them fight for the belt; especially since they were booked two times prior. This is a hard fight to call but much like Brady/Buckley, one I expect the winner to dominate. This is a classic striker vs grappler matchup and both guys have a clear hole in their game for the other to take advantage of. I’ve never picked against Taira and I love his grappling but I’m not sure I like this fight for him. Grappling has been one of Van’s weaknesses since the beginning of his career but I think he’s made a lot of improvements since then. He hasn’t been tested against someone at the level of Taira but I do believe he will make it hard to score takedowns here. Taira is an elite grappler but imo his wrestling still needs to come along. He’s still a very good wrestler but I don’t know if it’s fair to confidently say he’s made strides since the Royval fight. He dominated Park like he should’ve and finishing Moreno was impressive, but in terms of his wrestling he did not show me a lot of improvements. Moreno defended the takedown in r1 but decided to jump on a triangle and spent the whole round spamming it. Taira was holding his own on the feet in r2 but was still behind, and was struggling to get the takedown until he found the inside trip. This fight showed how lethal his grappling is but I also think it’s worth noting that Moreno is very washed currently; he looked off that entire fight, and I say this as someone who confidently backed Taira in that spot. Taira looked the same as always to me; not in a bad way, but I simply don’t know if he’s hit that next level from the Royval fight. I do expect him to get takedowns at some point here but personally I think Van is going to make it tough for him. Everyone tries to shoot on Van so it’s nothing new, and he has made a lot of improvements in his tdd; I thought Tsuruya was going to be able to outwrestle him but Van fought perfectly. Tsuruya is obviously not close to Taira’s level, but that fight at least showed me he can deal with a wrestling heavy attack. I really wish we could’ve seen the Pantoja fight play out to get a read on Van’s defensive grappling. Taira’s back/top control is some of the best but while fresh I do think Van can at least stay safe; I can’t say that with confidence though since there’s not much footage of him facing someone this good. That being said though, I really think Van will give Taira a hard time to get a takedown. Besides having good tdd he has great urgency to get to his feet. The problem here is avoiding the back take of Taira; that’s my main worry because the Royval fight showed he just needs a limb to get to your back and control you. Despite this, I thought Royval fought like a dumbass (like always), he kept giving Taira opportunities to get back into the fight because he was either throwing a lot of kicks or engaging in the grappling himself. He can definitely do that to Van but imo Van has gotten smarter and doesn’t take as many risks/do as much dumb **** as he used to. He won’t kick at all so it mostly comes down to defending the shots. Van also pressures heavily on the feet which I think will make it harder for Taira to score takedowns, as well as make the attempts more predictable. I’ve talked a lot about the grappling but it goes the exact opposite way on the feet; if Van keeps this standing I don’t think this is a close fight. Taira’s fundamentals are good and he’s always had natural power for the division but much like his wrestling, I don’t know if his striking has leveled up yet either. He did drop Park which was cool but against Moreno he wasn’t really doing much on the feet; he was holding his own but I wouldn’t say he was ahead. Van’s striking is going to be a big problem for him; it’s like the Royval fight except he has more power, much better technique, and is going to be ripping the body the entire fight. Taira actually has good cardio imo (he recovered insanely well in the Royval fight) and moving to Colorado for camps will only help that, but I think it’s fair to say he won’t be able to keep up with Van’s pace on the feet. His volume and aggression is just too much. Taira has the power to crack Van if he gets too ****y, but other than that I don’t see him having any success here. If it gets extended late I expect Van to take over heavily with his pace. The heart Taira showed in the Royval fight does make it scary if things go late, but imo those same opportunities won’t present themselves. If it goes late, I actually favor Van a lot. The devil’s advocate says that more time means more opportunities for Taira to get takedowns, but with Van’s constant body work and pressure, I think Taira is going to struggle. Royval could’ve finished him in r3 but he followed Taira to the ground and let him recover. This is a great and very close fight. Taira has the ability to end this fight off of a single takedown, but I think Van is the more complete MMA fighter. Taira’s grappling is very dangerous and we know Van struggles in that area, but as an underdog I feel like you have to go Van here. His tdd may not be tested against someone at this level but he’s clearly hard to take down and has an even bigger advantage on the feet.

Volkov vs Cortes-Acosta
Volkov DEC

HW has been on fire recently so I have high hopes for this one. However with both these guys' styles, I'm worried this turns into a pretty boring fight. Waldo has been knocking people out but when he faces more durable/technically sound guys his fights are usually terrible. I like Volkov but he’s had his fair share of absolute snoozers as well. Waldo has been on fire lately so I understand the appeal but personally I don’t think he has anything for Volkov. Waldo’s been knocking everyone out but he’s also fought three guys who are not durable, and three guys who willingly got in his face and brawled. It’s hard to remember because he fought so much, but Waldo isn’t even a year removed from the Pavlovich fight either. He did make it competitive but Pavlovich pretty much outclassed him the entire fight. Pav started off slow but once he found his groove he easily outboxed Waldo, and Waldo just sat at range taunting. Volkov doesn’t have the power or long gorilla arms of Pavlovich, but he has size and significantly better technical ability than him. Volkov is old but he’s in his prime right now, he has improved his grappling a lot and his striking has never looked better. I do think he should’ve lost to Jailton but I also thought he got robbed against Gane so that’s alright. The Jailton fight isn’t relevant though because Waldo’s wrestling is not on that level. If anything, Volkov has the ability to mix in takedowns here. I expect Volkov to strike with Waldo the entire time though, and he’s just simply much better than Waldo. I do think Waldo can make it competitive but Volkov is not going to stand and bang with him, and has all the tools to outhustle Waldo at range. He throws a lot of kicks which is something Waldo has always had trouble with (and hasn’t had to face since the Pezao fight) and honestly I think he’s a better boxer than Waldo too. Waldo’s volume is what makes him great but I think his punching is kind of sloppy. I will say though, he’s definitely improved his boxing; before he would loop his shots a lot but now he throws them more tight and has been sleeping people as a result. That won’t be easy against Volkov though, he has a chin and it will be difficult to hit him due to his height. I’m still not convinced Waldo is technical enough to beat elite heavyweights, Pavlovich outclassed him in r2/3 and while he’s looked good since, none of the guys he’s fought are close to top 5 level. I also still believe he lost to Spivac, and that was almost entirely striking. Waldo may win a round but I think Volkov is going to use his reach to work at Waldo from range and annoy him because he won’t step in the pocket. He has a very crisp jab and I also expect the leg/body kicks to be problematic for Waldo. He is tough but it’s going to frustrate him and hinder his movement. For Waldo to win this fight he either needs to show some massively improved wrestling, or get a knockout. The latter is possible but Volkov is insanely durable and Waldo’s last three wins haven’t convinced me that he’s magically a heavy hitter now.

Brady vs Buckley
Brady DEC

I’m pretty split on this one. I was feeling confident in Buckley before taping but after going through it, his wrestling/grappling is simply just not on the level of Brady’s. I still think he can win this fight and I also like that he did half of this camp at Goat Shed/Kill Cliff, but you can only improve your wrestling so much in the span of a year. Especially when fighting an elite grappler. Buckley’s tdd in the open is pretty good, as well as his first layer tdd but once you put him on his back he is not getting up. The Usman fight was a terrible look. I know he’s an elite wrestler and even in current form is still insanely skilled but he just completely bullied Buckley; he looked how he did in his prime. Buckley couldn’t even stop the initial shots and once grounded he simply had nothing for Usman. I was willing to let this slide because I think Usman is a better wrestler than Brady, but the Colby fight was a very bad look to me. Buckley’s tdd held up for the most part, but there were two moments I didn’t like: in round two, he got Buckley to the cage and finally committed on a good shot. He took Buckley down with little resistance and held him down for the remainder of the round. In r3 Buckley was outgrappling him but he got sloppy when he tried to take the back and got reversed, again cruising out the round on his back. Colby had nothing for him so I can understand conceding control, but when he and Usman are the only credible wrestlers Buckley’s fought on this WW run, it’s a little concerning. His tdd is good and he can definitely defend a few shots from Brady, but once he gets it to the cage it's over. Brady can wrestle out in the open but his cage wrestling is where he’s best; coincidentally, this is where Buckley struggles the most. The only takedown Colby scored came when he got Buckley’s back to the cage, and Usman was able to dominate the first 4 rounds because he kept pressuring him to the cage and shooting there. Buckley has been getting different looks and I'm sure has been wrestling his ass off, but I simply don't know if he can improve enough to stop the grappling onslaught. That’s not to say he can’t win though; if he is able to stop the takedowns he can (and likely will) dominate just as easily as Brady can on the ground. Brady is a serviceable striker and imo better than most people think, but he is uncomfortable and hates getting hit. I hate to say it because I know he specifically hired a sports psychiatrist to improve upon this, but Brady has some quit in him. We all know the Belal and Chiesa fights but he had nothing for Morales either. I don’t hold this loss against him at all, but after one bad takedown and one combo from Morales, Brady panicked and got finished in a round. His immediate response to the loss was “I’m gonna drop down to 155 pounds”, which I found so weird. It’s like he didn’t believe in his skills because he fought the biggest guy in the division. Skillswise Brady is great but he tends to fall apart when he can’t get his game going. He doesn’t have the power to expose Buckley’s bad habits on the feet, or the chin. Once Buckley lands a clean hook on him, he is going to be looking for the door. Buckley also has pretty insane cardio at 170 which can be an edge. However, in order to use that cardio you have to actually show resistance; if Brady takes him down and controls him all of r1, he’s going to rinse and repeat the entire fight. That’s literally his entire gameplan. I’m really split because each guy has clear holes in their game and clear strengths they can utilize. I still believe in Buckley at 170 but he hasn’t faced a ton of credible opponents and his bottom game/cage wrestling weakness is too glaring for me to ignore. I think this is going to look like the KG fight except I’m not sure if he subs Buckley. I also wouldn’t be surprised if Brady gets hit once and just completely folds to Buckley. Brady also came up short in his second title eliminator, and that worries me for his mentality; Buckley looks hungrier than ever on socials and finally went to a gym outside of Missouri so I do think he can improve. I think Brady will grind this one out though.

Green vs Stephens
Green KO RD 2

Another Stephens fight was not on my bingo card but I’m all here for it; he is not a serious fighter and is going to lose to 99% of the guys they match him with, but Stephens loves a scrap and is never in a bad fight. He’s also fighting Green, a fellow OG, so I’m expecting a great, throwback style fight. I thought Bobby was completely done but he has proven me wrong; he looked like he turned the clock back against Zellpooper. Now, it’s worth noting that was one of the worst performances of the year from Zell, and he may just be a fraud anyways but considering how bad Bobby looked prior to that, it was impressive. Regardless of current form, I expect Bobby to easily win this; there’s a difference between being an active fighter struggling against good comp, versus being a part time fighter coming back for paychecks. Stephens isn’t going to quit or half ass it, but it’s pretty obvious he is coming back to MMA for paychecks and entertainment. He’s shockingly not as washed as his 1-8-in his-last-9-record looks, but he is very much a checked out fighter. He’s been doing very well in BKFC, although he did just take an insane ass whooping last fight, so maybe age is starting to catch up everywhere. This is going to be an all striking affair and I expect Stephens to make it a scrap, but I don’t see him outpointing Green. Green’s style is unique, there’s really not anything like it. His hand down style has cost him a lot in the latter stages of his career but he’s still extremely crafty. His jab is money and once he finds his range on it, he just snipes you at range all fight. Bobby’s had some terrible looks but he doesn’t have any losses that I’d say are alarming just yet. Stephens is all the way done though imo. I know he made it a scrap against Jones but Jones is also someone who blocks punches with his face. Bobby’s defense has slowed in his older age but he’s still hard to hit. Stephens has all his experience but doing BKFC the last few years, he’s moved to a more boxing centric approach. I don’t think Bobby is going to hang out in the pocket and throw down with him, and at range I think Bobby takes over with his jab and constant output. Stephens is tough, won’t gas, and doesn’t have to worry about takedowns here, but imo that doesn’t matter because Bobby is simply just a better striker. I want to say decision since Stephens is tougher than a two dollar steak but I have a feeling Bobby could KO him. Probably wishful thinking since he lowkey has pillowfists but idk, once he gets the jab going he is going to land at will on Stephens. I know he’s very tough but Bobby’s output is going to be through the roof, and personally I don’t think Stephens can withstand a barrage of punishment. He ate shots from Jones well but 1. Jones is not a ko guy and 2. He immediately started wrestling once Stephens got the pressure going. Either way, I expect this to be a pretty clean win for Bobby. Stephens is always fun and an OG of the game but he is shot to pieces and honestly it's a bit confusing to me that he’s even still competing in the UFC; just do BKFC, the pay can't be that much different from the UFC lol.

posts.author: Fast_Tulip
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Carpenter vs. Ochoa
Clayton Carpenter, DEC
Such a tough fight to call, but I'm leaning Carpenter for now. If the fight stays standing for 3 rounds, Ochoa takes it by DEC. Carpenter has ate some big clean shots and it barely moved his head so I can't see him getting KOed. He looks and has the jaw of Drew Dober. Ochoa's biggest weakness is when he's on his back. When Almabayaev got him on his back, he was stuck there till the end of R2 and laid there for half a round, and was stuck on his back for 3 minutes in round 3. I'm not sure how many times Carpenter can shoot takedowns, how accurate they will be, and if the takedowns will put Jose on his back. There's a huge difference getting taken down against the cage vs taken down into full guard. The thing I like about Carpenter though is that his hands aren't too bad. I think he can get enough respect with his hands that can lead to takedowns. Carpenter's control from top is also pretty good. He had trouble with Tagir as Tagir just outsized him when grappling, and Filho sub was just unfortunate and creative from Filho. There's a chance Carpenter could get a sub as well, but I'm leaning towards decision for now

posts.author: flavorpacket
posts.content:
[Predictions]

Tulio vs Kopylov
Tulio, DEC

The difference between these two guys is Tulio will actually fight for your money for every second of the fight. Kopylov has good moments in every fight but lacks urgency and lets rounds slip away. I think we’ve already seen the best of Kopylov as someone who can beat most of the unranked guys but can’t really compete with the top 15. I do think Tulio has the potential to be ranked in the future mainly because of his win over Belgaroui which is aging well in the context of this matchup. Tulio has a lot to improve on skillwise. I think he can beat Kopylov with his aggressive forward pressure and physicality. It’s very hard to take down Kopylov but I wouldn’t be surprised if Tulio tries to mix in some grappling. Taking down Kopylov is the golden ticket to beating him imo it’s just hard as **** to do so. There’s obviously a chance Tulio gets knocked out again but I don’t think Kopylov is that dangerous. Even when Kopylov hurts his opponent he usually backs away and lets them off the hook. I think Tulio should be able to get past the gatekeeper here.

@JeanSilvaEnjoyer - All subjective but I really think Tulio is still that dude despite the CLD loss. What he showed in the Gore fight was substantially more impressive than almost anything Kopylov has done in the UFC thus far.

Too many times I've trusted Kopylov to come out and kickbox the brakes off of someone and he just... doesn't?

Kopylov is timid, low volume, can get backed up too easily and chooses to wait for the right moments to go rather than to just go.

I'm siding with Tulio as I think his pressure will nullify Kopylov's best tools, I think Kopylov's best chance at winning is to be superb in the counterstriking, but I will never trust that dude to do what he is "supposed" to do again.

I honestly don't even think Kopylov is that good anymore, maybe fringe lower Top 15, but he will never stiff Top 5 in his life as far as I see it.

Tulio however, I think if he tightens his defense up he can really start to make a run upwards. Nullifying Belgaroui's offense as he did on the Contender was a very promising sign, Belgaroui has grown exponentially since then but still, that's a very hard opponent to defeat due to size, reach and his striking prowess.

TLDR; Tulio has much more upside, think we've seen Kopylov's ceiling.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
[Predictions]

I’ve never gotten the Tulio hype. Outside of the Yousri win (he was suffering from Lyme Disease when they fought) he doesn’t really have any good wins. I really don’t understand why he’s -2xx over Kopylov either; besides his hype what has he done to justify that? He can wrestle but I don’t think it’s good enough to rely on here, his technique isn’t as good and he’s almost all boxing, his process isn’t anything special when he isn’t throwing down in the pocket, and he’s super hittable. I don’t think he’s very durable either, he’s been ko’ed completely unconcious in both of his losses, and the biggest red flag to me was the Gore fight; this was a domination but the 10 strikes Gore landed all had a huge effect. He managed to wobble Tulio momentarily. Tulio also slowed down heavily against CLD which is a big concern to me because I assume a large part of this line is due to the alleged cardio advantage. Experience is not the be all end all, but it does matter. The UFC’s prospect building path has recently been based on sink or swim; if they like you and you lose, the UFC usually pushes guys up the rankings with “harder” bounce back fights (ex. Silva vs Allen, Talbott vs Lima, Lone’er vs Moreno/Bruno Silva, Vologdin vs Castaneda, etc.). In most cases I back the prospect but I’m not sure if Tulio is ready to be pushed up the rankings. Like I said, the CLD fight was largely one sided and his two wins before that are against jobbers. I’ve never been a Kopylov guy but since he started to get things together, the only three guys to beat him are all ranked in the top 10 or higher; I know level of comp doesn’t mean much but Tulio is definitely not as good as the guys beating Kopylov lately

Van vs. Taira

This fight is tough to call because I don't think the takedowns will come easy for Taira. Ultimately I'm going with Taira though because the gap on the ground is extremely wide. Royval was able to survive against Taira because he is also a legitimate black belt. Van is a blue belt and still inexperienced on the ground, so all Taira could need is one takedown and body triangle to lock up the RNC and finish this fight. Whoever keeps it in their world will win this, but Van isn't a one punch KO guy, so I think he would have to keep it standing for at least a couple rounds to break Taira and get the TKO. In Taira's world on the ground he can end it very quickly. Give me Taira SUB R2 after facing some adversity in R1.
